title: Vortex pinning of Ba$_{0.62}$K$_{0.38}$BiO$_3$ investigated by magneto-optical Kerr-effect and magnetization measurements
authors: Soichiro Yamane, Sota Nakamura, Atsutoshi Ikeda, Dayu Zhai, Siddarth Gorregattu, Xing He, Sudarshan Sharma, Yipeng Cai, Yasutomo J. Uemura, Martin Greven, Shingo Yonezawa
abstract: Vortex pinning plays a crucial role in determining properties of type-II superconductors. For example, it governs the irreversible magnetic response as well as dissipation caused by vortex motion. Here, we study vortex pinning in the three-dimensional oxide superconductor Ba1-xKxBiO3 using ultra-high-resolution magneto-optical Kerr effect (MOKE) and detailed magnetization measurements. We find that the zero-field MOKE signal in the superconducting state exhibits a pronounced magnetic-history dependence. This behavior closely resembles the remanent magnetization caused by trapped vortices. Furthermore, we demonstrate that the observed evolution of the MOKE signals is well described by Bean's critical-state model for trapped vortices. Our results establish MOKE as a viable optical and mesoscopic probe of vortex pinning in type-II superconductors, providing a new complementary approach to investigate mixed-state phenomena. We also find that the training-field dependence of the MOKE is linear near zero training field, without any anomalies indicative of spontaneous time-reversal-symmetry breaking in an unconventional superconducting state. Our study defines a clear protocol to distinguish vortex-induced MOKE responses from those associated with a time-reversal-symmetry broken superconducting order parameter.
summary: This work establishes MOKE as a complementary, highly sensitive optical tool to study vortex pinning in type-II superconductors like $	ext{Ba}_{0.62}	ext{K}_{0.38}	ext{BiO}_3$. By comparing MOKE signals with bulk magnetization data using the critical-state model, the authors confirm that the observed magnetic history dependence is due to trapped vortices, not exotic superconducting order parameters.
main_problem: The study investigates vortex pinning, a critical mechanism governing irreversible magnetic response and energy dissipation in type-II superconductors.
main_result: The Magneto-Optical Kerr Effect (MOKE) proves to be a viable optical probe for studying vortex pinning, showing quantitative agreement with bulk magnetization measurements when analyzed via the Bean critical-state model.
method: The authors employed ultra-high-resolution MOKE and detailed DC SQUID magnetization measurements on $	ext{Ba}_{1-x}	ext{K}_x	ext{BiO}_3$ single crystals under various field training protocols.
model_or_system: The physical system is the oxide superconductor $	ext{Ba}_{0.62}	ext{K}_{0.38}	ext{BiO}_3$. The analysis relies heavily on adapting the Bean critical-state model to describe the remanent magnetic flux profile associated with trapped vortices.
key_observables: Zero-field MOKE signal ($\Delta	heta_K$), bulk magnetization ($M$ vs. $H_{	ext{eff}}$), and temperature dependence of susceptibility ($\chi$).
important_parameters: Critical current density ($j_c(T)$), penetration field scale ($\mu_0 H_p$), and the training magnetic field magnitude ($|H_T|$).
main_assumptions: The remanent response is modeled using generalized saturation functions $F_{	ext{Deff}}(h)$, which relate local (MOKE, $	ext{Deff}=0$) and bulk ($	ext{Deff}=2$) measurements to the critical-state model.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate crystal structure, MOKE setup schematics, temperature dependence of both $M$ and $\Delta	heta_K$, and quantitative comparisons showing how both local and bulk responses follow universal saturation functions derived from the critical-state theory.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by establishing the physical problem (vortex pinning), detailing the complementary experimental methods (MOKE vs. Magnetization), developing a unified theoretical framework using the Bean model, and finally applying this framework to quantitatively compare local and bulk measurements while ruling out alternative superconducting mechanisms like TRSB.
why_interesting: While focused on superconductivity, the rigorous application of universal scaling functions ($F_{	ext{Deff}}(h)$) derived from continuum models (like the critical-state model) provides a powerful template for analyzing mesoscopic responses in other condensed matter systems exhibiting flux trapping or domain wall dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 4. (a)(b) Magnetic-flux distribution B(r) arising from vortex pinning, as described by Bean’s critical-state model [2], for the field- training protocol used in this study. Here, Hp denotes the full flux penetration field in the phenomenological Bean-model picture. (a) When the training field satisfies |HT| ≳Hp, the final flux distribution B(r) at zero field is expected to be independent of HT. Upon warming, the reduction of jc leads to a uniform decrease of the slope of the linear B(r) profile. This leads to HT-independent remanent response as a function of temperature.
